coredata.dc:title: Novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots for “turn-on” sensing of ATP based on aggregation induced emission enhancement effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Yang
Wang, Xianfeng
Wu, Shangming
Shen, Jinhui
Ma, Wenhao
Yang, Siyi
Fa, Huanbao
Yang, Mei
Hou, Changjun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) was successfully synthesized by hydrothermal synthesis of polyethylenimine (PEI) and citric acid. The as-prepared CDs suffered from aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) with a high concentration, but after adding adenosine triphosphate (ATP), the CDs aggregated. The generation of aggregates caused the rotation of the surface groups on CDs and reduced the non-radiation decay. The QY of CDs in water was 9.25 %, and increased to 16.60 % and 63.38% in the addition of 100 and 1000 μM ATP. And then, the enhancement of the radiation rate led to the aggregation induced enhancement effect (AIEE). Moreover, we also found that the proportion of precursors for CDs synthesis was a key factor in the occurrence of AIEE. Therefore, such CDs would be excellent candidates as fluorescent probes for the label-free detection of ATP. Our proposed method exhibited simple and easy preparation of nanoprobe, quick response (3 min), wide range of linear rage (1–2000 μM) and eco-friendly. In addition, the method performed successfully as a “turn-on” sensor for detection of ATP in the tablet with a recovery of 100.1~106.9% and RSD below 3.5%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
ATP
Turn-on
AIEE

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ATP induced AIEE properties of CDs
Mechanism discussion of ATP induced AIEE properties of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of the obtained CDs in solution, insets: photographs of the CDs solutions under sunlight (left) and 365 nm ultraviolet radiation (right); (B) TEM images of CDs, inset: Particles size distribution measured by TEM; (C) FT-IR spectrum of CDs; XPS spectrum (D, E, F) of CDs, High resolution regions of C1s (D), O1s (E), and N1s (F).

Fluorescence spectra of CDs with different concentrations. (A) The concentration of CDs was 0.4, 0.8, 1.6, and 20 μg/mL; (B) The concentration of CDs was 20, 22, 25, 29, 40 μg/mL. The solvent of CDs is deionized water.

the Fluorescence spectra (A) and the characteristic phenomena (B) of CDs with different concentrations of ATP added (0 μM, 100 μM, 1000 μM) under the light of a laser pointer. The solvent of CDs is Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) and the concentration of CDs is 6.4 μg/ml.

The TEM images of CDs with different concentrations of ATP (0 μM (A), 100 μM (B), 1000 μM (C)); The TEM images of an aggregated CDs in the presence of 100 μM ATP (D) and the EDS results of the aggregated CDs refer to graphs (E) to (G), respectively was N, O, P element.

The property of CDs in the presence of ATP, zeta potential (A), FT-IR spectra (B) and full XPS spectrum (C); (D) The FL spectrum of three different CDs with ATP; (E) The FL spectrum of CD1 with the different value of pH in the presence of 20 μM ATP, insets: photographs of the CDs solutions with pH 4 (left) and pH 10 (right) under the light of a laser pointer. (F) The FL spectrum of three CDs with the different value of pH in the presence of 20 μM ATP. (Three different CDs refer to the different molar ratio of citric acid (CA) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) in the one-pot hydrothermal method. Three molar ratios of CDs were 1:1 (CD1), 10:1 (CD2) and 100:1 (CD3).
The UV–Vis absorption of CDs in the presence of ATP, (A) The absorption of CDs increased with the concentrations of ATP. (B) The absorption of CDs decreased with the concentrations of ATP.

(A) The fluorescence spectrum of CDs in the presence of ATP; (B) The corresponding calibration curve of CDs with the growing concentrations of ATP (0, 1, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 400, 800, 1200, 1600, 2000 μM).
The net fluorescence (ΔF) of CDs in the presence 80 μM of different metal ions and organic small molecular.
Nitrogen-doped CDs were prepared by one-pot method and ATP sensing based on the aggregation-induced emission enhancement effect.
The rate constants of the CDs in aqueous solution in the presence of ATP.
Detection of ATP in adenosine disodium triphosphate tablets.

head.ce:title: Novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots for “turn-on” sensing of ATP based on aggregation induced emission enhancement effect

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Biorheological Science and Technology (Chongqing University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) was successfully synthesized by hydrothermal synthesis of polyethylenimine (PEI) and citric acid. The as-prepared CDs suffered from aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) with a high concentration, but after adding adenosine triphosphate (ATP), the CDs aggregated. The generation of aggregates caused the rotation of the surface groups on CDs and reduced the non-radiation decay. The QY of CDs in water was 9.25 %, and increased to 16.60 % and 63.38% in the addition of 100 and 1000 μM ATP. And then, the enhancement of the radiation rate led to the aggregation induced enhancement effect (AIEE). Moreover, we also found that the proportion of precursors for CDs synthesis was a key factor in the occurrence of AIEE. Therefore, such CDs would be excellent candidates as fluorescent probes for the label-free detection of ATP. Our proposed method exhibited simple and easy preparation of nanoprobe, quick response (3 min), wide range of linear rage (1–2000 μM) and eco-friendly. In addition, the method performed successfully as a “turn-on” sensor for detection of ATP in the tablet with a recovery of 100.1~106.9% and RSD below 3.5%.

ce:section.ce:para: Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is an essential energy-rich phosphate compound in cells that provides energy for a variety of biological processes in cells, including energy transfer, neurotransmission, DNA replication, signal transduction, and so on [1]. At the same time, ATP concentrations are closely linked to certain diseases, such as cancer, cardiovascular disease, and hypoglycemia [2,3]. Therefore, the monitoring of ATP concentration is necessary and full of significant clinical value because it is an important indicator for disease diagnosis. Up to now, colorimetry [4–6], chemiluminescence[7,8], electrochemical [9,10], and fluorescence assays [11,12] has been proposed for ATP sensing. Among them, fluorescence method has attracted more attention because of the simple principle of design, rapid response, signal stability and other characteristics, and has been widely used. In fact, many fluorescent probes based on nanomaterials have been developed for ATP detection, such as quantum dots (QDs), Hetero-atom-doped QDs [13], carbon dots (CDs), Hetero-atom-doped CDs [14], and metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) [15]. However, most fluorescent probes are based on fluorescence quenching strategy, such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [16] and aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [17]. In general, fluorescent “turn-on” probes are preferable to “turn-off” for better selectivity and fewer false positive signals [18,19]. Therefore, it is very full of practical application value to develop a “turn-on” based open fluorescent probe to detect ATP.

To date, there have been some sensors based on “turn-on” sensing strategy through the design of aggregation induced emission enhancement (AIEE) to detect metal ions, small molecules, VOCs and other targets [20–23]. Tang’s group first reported AIEE phenomenon in 2001 [24]. Different from AIEE fluorophores in dissolved state with low concentration show very weak fluorescence emission, the formation of molecular aggregates under high concentration restricts the intramolecular motion, showing enhanced fluorescence emission [25,26]. In recent years, a number of AIEE luminogens have been developed focusing on organic molecules, including the distyrylbenzene, cyanostilbene, and pyrene derivatives [27–31]. The most reported the AIEE luminogens was organic dyestuff. Compared with the common small organic molecule, AIEE probes based on nanomaterials remain to be studied and developed.

Since the discovery of CDs by Scrivens et al in 2004 [32], CDs has aroused wide interest among researchers all over the world. Due to its advantages of easy synthesis, low toxicity, easy degradation, strong chemical inertness, good fluorescence stability, easy modification and adjustable optical properties [33], CDs has been widely used in sensing and detection [34]. Among CDs-based optical biosensors, the most commonly used sensing mechanisms are “turn-off” and “on-off-on” [33], and three photoluminescence -quenching (PL-quenching) mechanisms have been used in the design of CDs-based optical biosensors, such as FRET [35–37], photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [38], and inner filter effect (IFE) [39]. Unlike the “on-off” strategy, “turn-on” strategy is that the fluorescence of CDs turned off by a non-analyte quencher first and then restored by the addition of a target analyte [33]. To date, only a few reports have been reported for CDs-based AIEE mechanism for sensing detection [18,40,41]. Therefore, it is very necessary to develop CDs-based AIEE for “turn-on” fluorescent sensing.

In this paper, CDs was synthesized by simple one-pot hydrothermal method using PEI and citric acid as precursors, and DMF as solvent (Scheme 1 ). We found that the as-prepared CDs showed aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) phenomenon such fluorescence emission intensity of CDs would decrease at high concentration and the maximum emission summit would have a red shift. However, the CDs would aggregate after adding ATP through coordination and electro-static interaction of the molecules, contributing to AIEE. The AIEE phenomenon of the prepared CDs in the presence of ATP could be a new “turn-on” optical sensing probe to the detection of ATP in complex samples.

Ethylenediamine sealing side polyethyleneimine (PEI, Mw = 800), citric acid (CA), Adenosine triphosphate (ATP), adenosine monophosphate (AMP), and adenosine diphosphate (ADP) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company. Adenosine disodium triphosphate tablets (20 mg/tablet) were purchased from GuangZhou BaiYunShan Pharmaceutical Holdings CO.LTD. Other conventional chemicals with analytical grade were purchased from the Macklin Reagent Corporation. All reagents are analytically pure and are used without further purification. Deionized water with a resistivity of 18.25 KΩ was obtained from a Millipore purification instrument.

The morphology of CDs was characterized by TEM (Talos F200S, ThermoFisher). The functional groups on the surface of CDs were identified by a FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet iS50, ThermoFisher). The chemical compositions of CDs were analyzed by an XPS spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi, ThermoFisher). The fluorescence spectrum of CDs was measured by a fluorospectrophotometer (FL970, Techcomp). UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired using UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2700, Shimadzu). Zeta potentials and hydrodynamic size were measured by dynamic laser light scattering (Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern). Time-resolved FL spectra was measured by using a time-correlated-single-photocounting (TCSPC) system of a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh) and calculated the fluorescence lifetime of CDs [42].The absolute quantum yields (QYs) was obtained with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh). The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) was calculated as follows [43,44]:
Q Y =
∫
L
e m i s s i o n
∫
E
s o l v e n t
- ∫
E
s a m p l e

Where QY is the absolute quantum yield, Lemission is the photon numbers of the fluorescence emission of sample, and E solvent and E sample are photon numbers of solvent and sample excited by excitation light, respectively.

The amine-functionalized CDs were prepared according to the previous work with modifications [45]. Briefly, 2.1 g citric acid and 8 g PEI were added into 10 mL DMF and form a homogeneous solution by ultrasonic. The solution was then transferred into a 20 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for 12 h. Then the reaction reactor was cooled to room temperature, and the obtained solution was poured into methanol for precipitation. The supernatant was dialysed with dialysis membrane (1 K Dalton) for 24 h to remove the unreacted residual precursor substances. Finally, the part of purified CDs solution through oven-drying to calculate the concentration of CDs. The rest part of purified CDs solution as the stored solution was obtained and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Three different molar ratios of CDs were 1:1 (CD1), 10:1 (CD2) and 100:1 (CD3), which refers to the different molar ratio of citric acid (CA) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) in the one-pot hydrothermal method. The CDs refer to the CD1 if there is no special explanation.

CDs stock solution (160 mg/L) were prepared with the purified CDs solution (1.6 mg/mL) using pure water solution, and ultrasonic treatment for 5 min to obtain the uniform suspensions. Different reaction conditions were optimized including pH (2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10), the concentration of CDs (1.6, 3.2, 6.4, 8, 9.6 mg/L), the reaction temperature (20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70 °C) and the reaction time (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 min). Under the condition of the best optimization of detection, 20 μL CDs reserve solution was added to 450 μL Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) with 30 μL different concentrations of ATP. The final concentrations of ATP was 0.5, 1, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 400, 800, 1200, 1600, 2000, 2200, 3000, 4000 μM respectively. The resulting mixture (final sample volume: 500 μL) was incubated at 25 °C in a water bath for 3 min and the fluorescence intensity at 460 nm was recorded by photoluminescence spectrometer.

Besides ATP, 80 μM of different metal ions and organic small molecular including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, glucose, histidine, dopamine, PO4 3−, ADP and AMP were investigated to evaluate the selectivity of our proposed method. The procedure was the same as described in the procedure of ATP detection section except the different substances.

Three tablets (Adenosine disodium triphosphate tablets, 60 mg) were grinded completely and dissolved in 300 mL Tris-HCl buffer. Then ultrasonic dispersion uniformity. After three times centrifugation with 10,000 rpm for 15 min, the supernatant was stored at 4 °C for further use. This assay was the same as described in the procedure of ATP detection section, except that the real sample of the tablet is used instead of the ATP solution. The ultimate concentration of ATP was set as 33.04 μM in the solutions.

The morphology of CDs could be clearly displayed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1B, CDs were uniformly dispersed in the field of vision without obvious aggregation with the average particle size of 8.9 nm, which could also be verified by dynamic light scattering (DLS) results (Fig. S2). DLS results showed that the hydrodynamic diameter of CDs was 12 nm, which was slightly larger than that observed in TEM. DLS measured the hydrodynamic diameter, and the CDs had a layer of hydration on its surface, so the result was slightly larger.

The surface groups and structures of CDs were further analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1C, the FT-IR spectrum of the as-prepared CDs showed the following characteristics peaks. 1651 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CO and often referred to “amide I band ”. The occurrence of 1651 cm−1 suggests that PEI amino and carboxyl reaction of citric acid to form a new amide bond [48]. Further analysis of the related peaks revealed the NH stretching vibration peak located at 3445 cm−1 and the CN stretching vibration peak located at 1384 cm−1, known as the “amide III band”. The in-plane bending vibration of NH often referred to as the “amide Ⅱ band”, may be due to the blue shift to the vicinity of 1640 cm−1 when CDs is the associative state. The “amide Ⅱ band” is often covered by the amide I band [18,46]. The amide bond associated peaks confirmed the formation of the amide bond in CDs synthesis. The peaks of 2918 and 2849 cm−1 are the antisymmetric stretching vibration peaks and the symmetric stretching vibration peaks of CH alkyl [18,47]. The COC stretching vibration peak at 1148 cm−1 was attributed to the ester formed by the dehydration of intermolecular or intramolecular hydroxyl groups of citric acid [18].

XPS was further examined to explore the existence states of elements on the surface of the as-prepared CDs. As depict in Fig. S3, three obvious peaks were detected by full spectrum scanning, which were respectively attributed to O1s (530.8 eV), C1s (284.8 eV) and N1s (398.8 eV), showing the composition of C, O and N elements in CDs. Detailedly, as shown in Fig. 1D, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of the C1s band includes four peaks, which respectively represent four different C atomic states: CC (284.2 eV), CC (284.9 eV), CN (285.5 eV) and CO (287.3 eV). As show in Fig. 1E, two diffraction peaks at 531.7 eV and 30.8 eV in high resolution scans of O1s are assigned to COC and COH bonds, respectively. Fig. 1F shows two peaks at 398.6 and 399.7 eV in the spectrum of N1s, indicating the presence of pyridinic C3 N and pyrrolic C2 NH groups in PEI-N-CDs. [18,47] It is confirmed that N atom has been successfully doped into the Sp2 aromatic domain structure of CDs and the surface of CDs has been connected with PEI, which is consistent with the results of FTIR spectroscopy characterization. The results of FT-IR and XPS undoubtedly implied the successful synthesis of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ATP induced AIEE properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared CDs showed aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [49]. As seen in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared CDs increased clearly with increasing the concentration of CDs from 0.4 to 20 μg/mL. However, the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared CDs decreased and there was a red shift of PL emission from 460 nm to 530 nm with increasing the concentration of CDs from 20 to 40 μg/mL (Fig. 2B). This phenomenon was similar to the results in prior works [50], which were ascribed photons emitted after excitation are likely to be reabsorbed by the surface-state transitions over a wide energy range. Interestingly, the aqueous solution of CDs with white turbidity was observed in the presence of ATP while it displayed transparent in the absence of ATP (Fig. S4). Furthermore, there was a light-yellow color precipitation in the bottom of centrifuge tube in the presence of ATP by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 3 min, and with brighter blue fluorescence than supernatant (Fig. S4-C-b). It was a striking contrast to the aqueous solution of CDs in the absence of ATP by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 3 min (Fig. S4-C-a). The result indicated that aggregation and fluorescence enhancement of CDs occurred after the addition of ATP, which may cause by AIE phenomenon reported in the previous literature [18,24,51]. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of CDs with different concentrations of ATP was further measured. Fig. 3 A shows the PL intensity of CDs showed gradually enhanced fluorescence with the increase concentration of ATP from 100 to 1000 μM. Moreover, the aggregations of CDs were confirmed by the characteristic phenomena of CDs with different concentrations of ATP in Fig. 3B, the samples with ATP showed different degrees of Tyndall Effect, and the Tyndall Effect more distinct with the increase of ATP concentration. This indicated that CDs was aggregated under the addition of ATP. Combined with the previously observed fluorescence enhancement of CDs after the addition of ATP, we speculate that CDs was aggregated induced fluorescence enhancement effect (AIEE) under the action of ATP.

For clear to see what happened in the microcosmos, the morphology and size of CDs in the presence of ATP was measured by TEM. As shown in Fig. 4 , the aggregation of CDs happened in the presence of ATP confirms the previous rational speculation. The sizes of CDs aggregate with different concentrations of ATP were from 300 nm (Fig. 4B) to 1 μm (Fig. 4C). The results exhibited that the aggregation level of CDs in the presence of ATP was more than it in the absence of ATP, increasing with the higher concentrations of ATP. Moreover the EDS elemental analysis results of aggregated CDs in the presence of 100 μM ATP displayed O, N and P three elements (Fig. 4E to 4G). The presence of P element in the aggregated CDs could be predicted because it was assigned to ATP. These experimental results indicated that ATP can induce the aggregation of CDs and lead to the aggregation induced enhancement effect (AIEE).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism discussion of ATP induced AIEE properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the resultant CDs exhibited the tendency of aggregation and enhanced fluorescence in the presence of ATP. As seen in Fig. 5 A, the zeta potential of the resultant CDs was 17.5 mv. However, the zeta potential decreased to 8.99 mv and 4.46 mv after the addition of ATP with the concentration of 100 and 1000 μM. The change of zeta potential uncovered that the electro-static interaction was the important force of ATP to binding CDs, which was presumably originates from the positive electricity of CDs and the negative electricity of ATP. Moreover, the surface groups and structures of CDs after the addition of ATP were examined according to FT-IR measurement (Fig. 5B). The main peaks (CO, NH) occurred around 10 nm shift (1651–1644 cm−1, and 3445–3454 cm−1), while the peaks of CN, the antisymmetric stretching vibration peaks and the symmetric stretching vibration peaks of CH alkyl showed the almost same peak. The FT-IR results indicated ATP can bound to the CO and NH bonds of the resultant CDs, which was reported by the published paper [52]. In addition, the full XPS spectrum uncovered the level of ATP binding with the resultant CDs and the percentage of P in the aggregated CDs was 1.7 % (Fig. 5C). The FT-IR and XPS results uncovered the coordination interaction was also made a great contribution of the aggregation of CDs in the presence of ATP [40,51,52].

Then, three different CDs were synthesized to further reveal the possible reasons of ATP induced AIEE properties. Three different CDs refer to the different molar ratios of citric acid (CA) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) used in the one-pot hydrothermal method. Three molar ratios of CDs were 1:1 (CD1), 10:1 (CD2) and 100:1 (CD3). As shown in Fig. 5D, CD1 showed the enhanced fluorescence with the addition of ATP, while no obvious change of fluorescence was observed in CD2. In contrast, CD3 even showed little decay of fluorescence in the presence of ATP. The results of different changes of fluorescence for the three CDs in the presence of ATP illustrated that the specific property provided by different molar ratios of CA:PEI, which was ascribed to the different surface electric charge of CDs. The low ratio of CA:PEI would lead to positive electricity and abundant amino groups (the primary amine, secondary amine and tertiary amine groups) on the surface of as-prepared CDs than the high ratio of CA:PEI in the synthesized procedure. The FL intensity of CD1 increased with the addition of ATP, which was presumably originates from the aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) by the electro-static interaction.

The CDs can be regarded as ampholyte for the carboxyl groups and amino groups on the surface of CDs. The different surface charge of the CDs can be obtained by adjust the pH of solutions. Herein, we speculated that the positive electricity of three CDs can be obtained by adjust the pH even three CDs had different isoelectric point (PI). More interestingly, CD1 exhibited the decay of fluorescence with the addition of 20 μM ATP at pH greater than 8 (Fig. 5E) and the CDs solutions with pH 10 depicted weak Tyndall Effect under the light of a laser pointer (right of the inset in Fig. 5E). While the fluorescence of CD1 enhanced with the addition of 20 μM ATP with pH less than 8, and the CDs solutions with pH 4 depicted obvious Tyndall Effect (left of the inset in Fig. 5E). As shown in Fig. 5F, CD2 and CD3 all showed the same tendency in the fluorescence response with the change of pH. The results illustrated the surface charge of CDs could leads to different of fluorescence response and the Tyndall Effect in the presence of ATP. All above results showed the CDs can be binding with ATP by coordination and electro-static interaction.

In order to further investigate the secret of enhanced fluorescence after the addition of ATP, the UV–Vis absorption, the fluorescence lifetimes and the absolute quantum yields (QY) of CDs were measured. As seen in Fig. 6 A, the UV–Vis absorption of CDs enhanced after the addition of ATP from 0 μM to 100 μM, which increased in the photon absorption and the fluorescence intensity of CDs. As shown in Fig. 6B, the absorption of CDs decreased when the concentration of ATP was greater than 100 μM, presumably originating from the CDs inside the aggregations that cannot absorb the photons. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs increased with the additions of ATP, which were 4.96, 7.15 and 9.06 ns in the concentration of 0, 100 and 1000 μM ATP respectively (Fig. S5). The QY of CDs in water was 9.25 %, and increased to 16.60 % and 63.38% in the addition of 100 and 1000 μM ATP (Table 1). Therefore, the radiative rate constants and the non-radiative rate can be calculated according the following equation: [53]
(1)
K r
=
Φ τ
(2)
K
nr
=
1 - Φ
τ

Wherein Kr and Knr is the abbreviation of the radiative rate constants and the non-radiative rate, respectively. Φ refers to the absolute quantum yields (QY), which indicate the PL efficiency. And with τ refers to the fluorescence lifetimes.

As shown in Table 1, The radiative rate constants of CDs in water was 18.6 × 106 s−1, which increased to 23.2 × 106 s−1 and 70.0 × 106 s−1 in the addition of 100 and 1000 μM ATP. Moreover, The non-radiative rate constants of CDs decreased from 1.17 × 108 s−1 to 0.40 × 108 s−1 when the addition of ATP form 100 to 1000 μM while the non-radiative rate constants of CDs in water was 1.83 × 108 s−1. The results illustrated the surface chemical groups of CDs were hampered by the aggregation of CDs in the presence of ATP. The occurrence of AIEE phenomenon in the presence of ATP was ascribed to the decay of non-radiative of aggregation of CDs [18,52].

Based on the highly selective and sensitive aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) phenomenon, we developed a facile method for “turn-on” fluorometric detection of ATP with the as-prepared CDs. As seen in Fig. 3, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs enhanced regularly with the addition of ATP after 100 to 1000 μM, which indicated the as-prepared CDs could be used in the detection of ATP. To optimizing the sensing performances, different reaction conditions were investigated including pH, the reaction time, the concentration of CDs and the reaction temperature. Firstly, we set pH = 7 for the following experiments in the detection of ATP based on the wide range of sensing and proper high sensitivity in the process of sensing and easy application in real sample (Fig. 5E). And then, 6.4 mg/L, room temperature and 3 min were selected as the optimum concentration of CDs, reaction temperature and reaction time for subsequent experiments (Fig. S7).

Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity was recorded at the emission of 460 nm to evaluate the performance of the presented method. As seen in Fig. 7 A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was enhanced regularly with the addition of ATP from 1 to 2000 μM. And the corresponding calibration curve of the fluorescence intensity of CDs with the increasing concentrations of ATP was obtained via the linear fitting (Fig. 7B). The two linear ranges were obtained in our proposed method. The calibration equation in 1–100 μM and 100–2000 μM was ΔF = 386.8 + 112.3C (R2 = 0.9902) and ΔF = 10722.9 + 8.5C (R2 = 0.9951) respectively. The two linear segments suggest that there are two reaction dynamic processes in the detection of ATP. Compared with the second linear segment (100–2000 μM), the first linear segment (1–100 μM) is steeper, which could be illustrated by the UV–Vis absorption of CDs in the presence of ATP (Fig. 6). As mentioned in the mechanism discussion section, the UV–visible absorption of CDs decreased when the concentration of ATP was greater than 100 μM, accompanied by the attenuation of photon absorption and fluorescence change rate. The limit of detection (LOD) of our presented method was 0.81 μM after calculation with the equation of 3σ/S. As shown in Table S1, compared with other reports, our proposed method exhibited simple and easy preparation of nanoprobe, quick response, wide range of linear rage and eco-friendly. Besides ATP, the net fluorescence (ΔF) of CDs in the presence of 80 μM of different metal ions and organic small molecular were investigated to evaluate the selectivity of our proposed method. As shown in Fig. 8 , when added the common metal ions such as Na+, K+, Mg2+ and Ca2+, it had slight effects on the fluorescence of CDs. And the fluorescence of CDs showed negligible increase on the introduction of glucose and PO4 3−. In contrast, the introduction of histidine and dopamine resulted in noticeable decrease of the fluorescence of CDs. And the ΔF of CDs in the presence of ADP and AMP which have the similar structures to ATP were also observed. The ΔF of CDs in the presence of AMP was nearly one fifth of the ΔF of CDs in the presence of ATP. While the ΔF of CDs in the presence of ADP was a notable increase contrast with the AMP and a quarter of the ΔF of CDs in the presence of ATP. Thus, we speculated that the ΔF of CDs was closely related to the number of phosphate groups in adenosines. However, the ATP level of samples with complicate base is greater than other adenosines such as ADP and AMP, which the negligible effects could be ignored in the procedure of sensing. Furthermore, the ΔF of CDs in the presence of ATP mixed with other interferents is near the ΔF of CDs in the presence of ATP alone, which shows the strong anti-interference ability. Thus, the proposed “turn-on” fluorescence assay can be used in a further ATP sensing.

Moreover, the adenosine disodium triphosphate tablets (20 mg/tablet) were selected to prove the feasibility of our proposed assay in real sample. As shown in Table 2 , The ultimate concentration of ATP was set as 33.04 μM in the solutions. The concentration of ATP is 33.53 μM (n = 3, RSD = 1.1%) in the procedure of detection and the quantality is 20.30 mg via calculation, which was acceptable contrast with the labeled amount. Meanwhile, the standard addition test results illustrated the recoveries of ATP were 100.1–106.9% with RSD below 3.5%., which is satisfactory. The results revealed our proposed method has the potential application in real sample.

In summary, a novel kind of nitrogen-doped CDs was successfully synthesized via controlling the ratio of precursors of CA and PEI. The CDs has the aggregation induced enhancement effect (AIEE) phenomenon induced by ATP, ascribing to the coordination and electro-static interaction between ATP and the as-prepared CDs. And the occurrence of aggregation of CDs with the restriction of the surface chemical groups resulted in the decay of non-radiative and the enhancement of fluorescence intensity. Based on the AIEE phenomenon, a facile method for “turn-on” fluorometric detection of ATP was established. Under the optimal conditions, the wide linear range of 1–2000 μM and the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.81 μM were obtained. And then, we successful realized the detection of ATP in adenosine disodium triphosphate tablets with satisfactory recovery. Therefore, the established of the fluorometric sensor based on AIEE is provide a quick response (3 min), wide range of linear rage, new facile and cost-effective method based on “turn-on” strategy, showing great potential for broad application in the sensing of ATP in complex samples.

Yang Huang: Conceptualization, Investigation, writing-original draft. Xianfeng Wang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Shangming Wu: Resources. Jinhui Shen: Validation, Data curation. Wenhao Ma: Visualization. Siyi Yang: . Huanbao Fa: Supervision. Mei Yang: Project administration, Funding acquisition, writing-review & editing. Changjun Hou: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 81271930 and 31000425), the Research Project of Chongqing Science and Technology Commission of China (cstc2020jcyj-msxmX0861), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2020CDJ-LHZZ-033), and we would like to thank Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University for SEM and TEM images, FT-IR and XPS spectrum, and zeta potential test.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121044.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hybridization chain reaction-based colorimetric aptasensor of adenosine 5′-triphosphate on unmodified gold nanoparticles and two label-free hairpin probes
